Article.ArticleTitle: Environmental fate of phthalate acid esters in shale gas exploitation areas: Adsorption, degradation, and vadose zone transport dynamics.

Abstract.AbstractText: Phthalate acid esters (PAEs) are process-specific contaminants in shale gas exploitation, yet their fate in heterogeneous vadose zones remains poorly understood. This study presents the first side-by-side comparison of four PAEs (Dimethyl phthalate (DMP), Diethyl phthalate (DEP), Dibutyl phthalate (DBP), Di(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate (DEHP) in two soil textures (Sandy loam, SL; Sandy Clay Loam, SCL) from a shale gas site, evaluating their adsorption, degradation, and transport behavior. Source apportionment revealed that shale gas exploitation activity acts as dominant contributors of soil DEHP, whereas PAEs with smaller molecular weight than DEHP were mainly stem from agricultural/domestic inputs. DEHP exhibited the highest concentrations (3.1 mg/L in flowback; 13 μg/g in soil) and longest persistence (half-lives ∼150-260 days) due to strong sorption capacity, whereas DMP showed minimal retention and faster degradation with T50 = 38-70 days. Leaching experiments demonstrated rapid breakthrough of DMP and DEP but pronounced retardation of DBP and especially DEHP (mobile fraction <0.1 in SCL). In the SL column, DEHP broke through earlier and reached a peak concentration roughly twice that measured in the SCL column. Modeling confirmed that coarse SL permits faster PAEs migration but shorter persistence, while fine SCL delays arrival yet prolongs low-level leaching. Critically, simulated DEHP in SL exceeded the 3 μg/L drinking water limit, underscoring groundwater contamination risks. These findings highlight an under-recognized risk of PAE pollution in shale gas areas and the need to consider compound-specific behaviors and soil heterogeneity in environmental risk assessments.
